# **Multilocus Species-Tree Reconstruction Reveals Deep Lineage Structure in East African Dwarf Galagos (*Paragalago*)**

**Authors:** Johan Karlsson, Andrew Perkin, Cristiano Vernesi, Luca Pozzi, Simon K. Bearder  
**Corresponding Author:** Johan Karlsson Email: [johan@kombagis.se](mailto:johan@kombagis.se)

---

# **Abstract**

Dwarf galagos (*Paragalago*) are small nocturnal primates endemic to eastern Africa, distributed across island, montane, coastal, and miombo woodland habitats. Their taxonomy and biogeographic history remain only partly resolved, particularly for southern populations in Malawi. Here, we reconstruct a multilocus species tree for Paragalago using six loci (mitochondrial 12S, 16S, and CYTB; nuclear IRBP, vWF intron 11, and vWF exon 28\) and a multispecies coalescent framework (StarBEAST3). The dataset comprises 24 individuals from Zanzibar, coastal and Eastern Arc forests in Tanzania, and, for the first time, dwarf galagos from Malawi, using *Otolemur garnettii* as the outgroup taxon.

The maximum clade credibility tree recovers four well-supported regional lineages: (1) *P. zanzibaricus zanzibaricus* from Zanzibar Island, (2) *P. z. udzungwensis* from mainland Tanzania, (3) *P. rondoensis* from the southern coastal forests, and (4) a distinct Malawi lineage, here referred to as *Paragalago cf. granti/nyasae*. All internal nodes show very high posterior support (≥ 0.9998). In relative time, *P. rondoensis* forms the deepest ingroup split, the Malawi lineage diverges next, and the shallowest divergence separates the Zanzibar and Udzungwa subspecies (\~3% of total tree height). This pattern is consistent with long-term isolation of the Rondo Plateau, a distinct southern miombo–montane refugium in Malawi, and more recent island–mainland divergence within the *P. zanzibaricus* complex.

Because the historical *P. nyasae* holotype from Mount Mulanje has been synonymized with *P. granti*, and no comparative sequences from confirmed *P. granti* populations are yet available, the precise taxonomic status of the Malawi lineage remains unresolved. Nevertheless, our results demonstrate that southern Malawi harbours a genetically distinct *Paragalago* lineage and provide the first molecular framework linking these populations to the wider East African dwarf galago radiation. The combined phylogenetic and biogeographic evidence underscores the importance of conserving isolated coastal, montane, and miombo forest fragments that together preserve the evolutionary diversity of *Paragalago*.

---

# **Introduction**

Dwarf galagos (genus *Paragalago*) represent one of East Africa's most geographically structured primate radiations (Butynski et al., 2006; Masters et al., 2017). Distributed across island, montane, coastal, and miombo woodland ecosystems (Butynski et al., 2006), their diversification has been shaped by repeated Pleistocene forest contractions, island–mainland isolation, and the long-term persistence of Afromontane refugia (Lovett, 1993; Bowie et al., 2006). Despite their ecological and evolutionary significance, the taxonomy of *Paragalago* remains complex and in some cases unresolved, particularly among populations in Malawi and Mozambique (Penna & Pozzi, 2024; Masters et al., 2017).

The genus *Paragalago* was recently erected to accommodate the eastern dwarf galagos previously classified within *Galagoides* (Masters et al., 2017). This taxonomic revision was based on comprehensive molecular phylogenetic analyses demonstrating that eastern African dwarf galagos form a distinct clade separate from western and central African *Galagoides* species (Masters et al., 2017; Pozzi et al., 2015). Within the traditionally recognized *P. zanzibaricus* complex, two subspecies have been described: the island form *P. z. zanzibaricus* (Matschie, 1893\) from Unguja (Zanzibar), and the mainland form *P. z. udzungwensis* (Honess, 1996\) from the Eastern Arc Mountains. Additional distinct lineages include *P. rondoensis* (Honess, 1996\) of the southern Tanzanian coastal forests and *P. granti* (Thomas & Wroughton, 1907), historically described from Mozambique and later associated with Malawi populations.

Species-level diversity within dwarf galagos has been revealed primarily through acoustic analysis of advertising calls, which serve as reliable taxonomic characters in this nocturnal group (Honess & Bearder, 1996; Butynski et al., 2006; Bearder et al., 2003). Molecular phylogenetic studies have largely corroborated acoustic-based taxonomic hypotheses, while also revealing cryptic diversity and complex biogeographic patterns (Masters et al., 2017; Pozzi et al., 2015). Previous molecular work on *Paragalago* has employed mitochondrial markers such as cytochrome b and 12S rRNA, as well as nuclear loci including IRBP and vWF, to resolve relationships among coastal and montane lineages (Masters et al., 2017; Pozzi et al., 2015).

A longstanding taxonomic uncertainty concerns dwarf galagos from southern Malawi. The holotype of *Galago nyasae* was collected on Mount Mulanje in 1907 (NHMUK 1908.5.29.22) and described by Thomas (1908), but was later synonymized with *Paragalago granti* based primarily on morphological comparisons (Honess, 1996). Field surveys in Malawi based on vocalizations and behavioural observations identified northern populations as *Paragalago zanzibaricus*, while leaving unresolved whether they correspond to the subspecies *P. z. zanzibaricus* or *P. z. granti* (**Courtenay & Bearder, 1989**). Subsequent acoustic surveys documented call types consistent with *P. granti* across several sites in southern Malawi (Honess & Bearder, 1996), yet many areas remain poorly surveyed and the relationship between Malawi dwarf galagos, *P. granti*, and the historical taxon *P. nyasae* remains unclear. Until now, no genetic samples from Malawi dwarf galagos have been included in molecular phylogenetic studies, leaving the evolutionary placement of these populations unresolved.

Here we reconstruct a multilocus species tree for dwarf galagos using mitochondrial (12S, 16S, CYTB) and nuclear (IRBP, vWF intron 11, vWF exon 28\) loci within a multispecies coalescent framework (StarBEAST3; Ogilvie et al., 2017). The multispecies coalescent explicitly models incomplete lineage sorting and provides more accurate estimates of species relationships than concatenated analyses, particularly for recently diverged taxa (Degnan & Rosenberg, 2009; Edwards, 2009). We include sequences representing Zanzibar, the Eastern Arc Mountains, the southern coastal forests (Rondo Plateau), and for the first time, dwarf galagos from Malawi. Because the taxonomic identity of the Malawi samples is unresolved, we conservatively refer to this lineage as ***Paragalago cf. granti/nyasae***.

Our objective is to resolve lineage structure within *Paragalago*, clarify the biogeographic history of dwarf galagos in eastern Africa, and place the newly sampled Malawi lineage within a broader phylogenetic context.

---

# **STUDY AREA**

Sampling for this study was conducted across three biogeographically distinct regions that together capture the major axes of divergence within *Paragalago zanzibaricus* and its close relatives. The sampled regions correspond to: (1) **Zanzibar Island**, representing the island subspecies; (2) **Mainland coastal forests of Tanzania**, representing the mainland subspecies; and (3) **Southern Malawi**, representing a historically poorly known dwarf galago lineage.

---

## **1\. Zanzibar Island — Kiwengwa Forest (Tanzania)**

Samples of *Paragalago zanzibaricus zanzibaricus* were collected from Kiwengwa Forest Reserve, a coastal coral-rag forest located along Zanzibar's eastern coast. Vegetation in this region is characterised by mixed coral-rag thickets, mangroves, and anthropogenic mosaics (Butynski et al., 2006). Zanzibar Island has been repeatedly isolated from mainland East Africa by fluctuating sea levels during Pleistocene glacial cycles, promoting the long-term genetic separation of the island subspecies from mainland *P. zanzibaricus* populations (Voelker et al., 2013).

**Representative population:** *P. z. zanzibaricus*  
**Habitat type:** Coral-rag coastal forest and thicket  
**Biogeographic context:** Oceanic isolation during Pleistocene interglacial periods

---

## **2\. Mainland Tanzania — Ruvu South & Ngumburuni Forest Reserves**

Mainland representatives of *Paragalago zanzibaricus udzungwensis* were sampled not from the montane Eastern Arc, but specifically from two lowland coastal forest reserves in southeastern Tanzania:

* **Ruvu South Forest Reserve**  
* **Ngumburuni Forest Reserve**

Both reserves form part of the **Swahili Coastal Forests**, a highly fragmented lowland forest mosaic distinct from montane Eastern Arc habitats (Burgess & Clarke, 2000). Although *P. z. udzungwensis* is broadly distributed across mainland forests associated with the Eastern Arc–coastal ecotone (Honess, 1996; Butynski et al., 2006). The populations sequenced here are specifically from low-elevation coastal forests, representing the mainland subspecies in the southern portion of its range.

**Representative population:** *P. z. udzungwensis*  
**Habitat type:** Lowland coastal forest mosaics  
**Biogeographic context:** Part of the wider Swahili–Eastern Arc biocomplex shaped by Pleistocene fragmentation (Lovett, 1993\)

---

## **3\. Southern Malawi — Blantyre, Mulanje, and Surrounding Miombo–Forest Mosaics**

Southern Malawi dwarf galagos had not been genetically sampled prior to this study. The only historical material consists of the holotype and paratype of \*Galago nyasae\*, collected on Mount Mulanje in 1907 (NHMUK 1908.5.29.22–23; Thomas, 1908). The two individuals sequenced here originated from households in the Blantyre region, where dwarf galagos are sometimes kept as pets captured from nearby forest patches. Although the precise capture locations are unknown, the animals almost certainly originated from the surrounding miombo woodland–forest mosaic landscape that characterizes southern Malawi

Previous nocturnal surveys conducted in Malawi documented vocalizations consistent with *Paragalago granti* at multiple sites in the region [(Courtenay and Bearder, 1989; Honess and Bearder, 1996\)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?m8usvz). However, these surveys relied primarily on acoustic identification and did not include genetic sampling. As a result, it remains unclear whether dwarf galagos in southern Malawi correspond to *P. granti*, the historical taxon *P. nyasae*, or a distinct lineage within the *Paragalago* radiation. For this reason, we refer to the Malawi lineage conservatively as *Paragalago cf. granti/nyasae*.

***Paragalago cf. granti/nyasae***

**Representative population:** *Paragalago cf. granti/nyasae*  
**Habitat type:** Miombo woodland–evergreen forest mosaics  
**Biogeographic context:** Southern edge of East African forest systems; potential historical refugium or contact zone (White, 1983\)

---

## Summary

This geographically structured sampling framework captures three major lineages relevant to the evolution of dwarf galagos in East Africa:

1. **Island lineage:** Zanzibar (*P. z. zanzibaricus*)  
2. **Mainland coastal lineage:** Tanzania coastal forests (*P. z. udzungwensis*)  
3. **Southern lineage:** Malawi (*P. cf. granti/nyasae*)

Each region represents a distinct ecological and biogeographic context, allowing us to test hypotheses related to island isolation, coastal forest fragmentation, and southern miombo divergence (Lovett, 1993; Bowie et al., 2006).

Sampling localities for all individuals included in the phylogenetic analysis are listed in Table S1 and shown in Figure 1\.

### ![][image1]

#### Figure 1\. Sampling localities of *Paragalago* individuals included in this study.

Map showing the geographic origin of samples included in the phylogenetic analyses. Sites represent three regional lineages: Zanzibar Island (*Paragalago zanzibaricus zanzibaricus*), mainland coastal forests of Tanzania (*P. z. udzungwensis*), and southern Malawi (*Paragalago cf. granti/nyasae*). Sampling locations are Kiwengwa Forest Reserve, Zanzibar (6.0056°S, 39.3770°E); Ruvu South Forest Reserve (6.9669°S, 38.8628°E); Ngumburuni Forest Reserve (7.8872°S, 39.0489°E); and the Blantyre region of southern Malawi (16.0526°S, 35.3713°E). Coordinates are given in WGS84 decimal degrees.

# **Methods**

## **Sample Collection and DNA Extraction**

Tissue samples were collected from 24 individuals representing four *Paragalago* lineages and one outgroup (*Otolemur garnettii*).

**Tanzania:** Samples from Zanzibar and mainland Tanzania (Ruvu South Forest Reserve, Ngumburuni Forest Reserve) were obtained during field surveys conducted between 2005 and 2011 under research permits issued by the Tanzania Wildlife Research Institute (TAWIRI, permit dated 9 May 2011\) and research clearance from the Tanzania Commission for Science and Technology (COSTECH, clearance issued June 2011 for a 12-month period, project title: "Molecular Phylogeny of East African Dwarf Galagos"). Field research was conducted in the regions of Tanga, Kagera, Lindi, Morogoro, Pwani, and Ruvuma. Sample export was authorized under CITES permit number 24905 (issued 4 October 2012 by the Wildlife Division, Dar es Salaam).

**Malawi:** Samples (n=2) were obtained from captive individuals (pet animals named "LOOPY" and "ZIT") kept in Blantyre during a pilot field survey conducted August–October 2007, with authorization from the Malawi High Commission (reference LC/2/2/24, dated 7 March 2007). These individuals likely originated from forest patches within the broader Blantyre–Mulanje region of southern Malawi.

All sampling procedures followed ethical guidelines for non-invasive tissue collection from small primates. The research project was conducted under the supervision of Prof. Simon K. Bearder (Oxford Brookes University, UK) as part of the Nocturnal Primate Research Group.

Genomic DNA was extracted from tissue samples using standard phenol-chloroform extraction protocols or commercial DNA extraction kits. DNA quality and concentration were assessed using spectrophotometry and gel electrophoresis prior to PCR amplification.

## **Molecular Markers and PCR Amplification**

Six genetic loci were amplified and sequenced:

* **Mitochondrial:** 12S rRNA, 16S rRNA, cytochrome b (CYTB)  
* **Nuclear:** interphotoreceptor retinoid binding protein (IRBP), von Willebrand factor intron 11 (vWF intron 11), von Willebrand factor exon 28 (vWF exon 28\)

These markers have been widely used in primate phylogenetics and provide complementary information from maternally inherited mitochondrial DNA and biparentally inherited nuclear loci (Masters et al., 2017; Pozzi et al., 2015). PCR amplification was performed using published primer sets and standard cycling conditions. PCR products were purified and sequenced bidirectionally using Sanger sequencing.

## **Sequence Alignment**

Sequences were aligned using **MUSCLE v3.8** (Edgar, 2004\) with default settings. Alignments were visually inspected and manually edited where necessary to correct obvious alignment errors. Ambiguous regions at alignment termini were trimmed to reduce phylogenetic noise. Final alignment lengths were: 12S (397 bp), 16S (212 bp), CYTB (423 bp), IRBP (604 bp), vWF intron 11 (567 bp), and vWF exon 28 (510 bp).

## **Table 1\. PCR Primers Used in This Study**

| Gene | Primer Name | Direction | Sequence (5' → 3') | Fragment Length | Reference |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| 12S rRNA | Ga\_12S-L1091 | Forward | CTGGGATTAGATACCCCACTAT | 397 bp | DelPero et al., 2000 |
| 12S rRNA | Ga\_12S-H1478 | Reverse | GAGGGTGACGGGCGGTGTGT |  | DelPero et al., 2000 |
| 16S rRNA | Ga\_16S-VF | Forward | TACATAACACGAGAAGACC | 213 bp | DelPero et al., 2000 |
| 16S rRNA | Ga\_16S-VR | Reverse | GTGATTGCGCTGTTATCC |  | DelPero et al., 2000 |
| CYTB | Ga\_CYTB\_L14724 | Forward | CGAAGCTTGATATGAAAAACCATCGTTG | 423 bp | Yoder et al., 1996 |
| CYTB | Ga\_CYTB\_H15149 | Reverse | CTCAGAATGATATTTGTCCTCA |  | Yoder et al., 1996 |
| IRBP | Ga\_IRBP\_532-F | Forward | GAGGTATGGTGCSGACAA\* | 604 bp | This study |
| IRBP | Ga\_IRBP\_1171-R | Reverse | CACTGACACTTGGAACAC |  | This study |
| vWF exon 28 | Ga\_vWFes28\_55\_F | Forward | ACCCCATATGTGGARGACA\* | 510 bp | This study |
| vWF exon 28 | Ga\_vWFes28\_669\_R | Reverse | AAGGTCACAGAGGTAGCTGA |  | This study |
| vWF intron 11 | Ga\_vWFin11\_112-F | Forward | CAGGCCATTGCTTTCTGT | 567 bp | This study |
| vWF intron 11 | Ga\_vWFin11\_787-R | Reverse | AGCCGAATGGTCACTGAG |  | This study |

\*IUPAC ambiguity codes: S \= G or C; R \= A or G

## **Model Selection**

For each locus, the best-fit nucleotide substitution model was determined using **jModelTest2 v2.1.10** (Darriba et al., 2012), evaluated under the Bayesian Information Criterion (BIC). All mitochondrial loci were assigned **HKY+Γ** (Hasegawa-Kishino-Yano model with gamma-distributed rate heterogeneity), and nuclear loci were assigned **HKY+Γ** where available in BEAST, HKY was approximated by HKY-like models.

## **Phylogenetic Analysis: Multispecies Coalescent**

We used **StarBEAST3** (Ogilvie et al., 2017\) implemented in BEAST 2.7.8 (Bouckaert et al., 2019\) to estimate the species tree under the multispecies coalescent model. This approach explicitly accounts for incomplete lineage sorting by modeling gene tree–species tree discordance and has been shown to provide more accurate species tree estimates than concatenated analyses, particularly for recently diverged taxa (Degnan & Rosenberg, 2009; Edwards, 2009).

The analysis was configured with:

* Separate gene trees for each locus  
* Linked species tree  
* Uncorrelated relaxed clocks (Drummond et al., 2006\) for each locus  
* Clock rate priors reflecting expected differences between mitochondrial and nuclear substitution rates  
* Broad priors on divergence times due to the absence of fossil calibrations

MCMC settings for the final production run:

* **200 million generations**  
* **Sampling every 20,000 steps**  
* **10,000 posterior samples**  
* Convergence assessed in **Tracer v1.7** (Rambaut et al., 2018\) with ESS \> 200 for all key parameters

The maximum clade credibility (MCC) tree was generated in **TreeAnnotator v2.7.8** using mean node heights and 95% highest posterior density (HPD) intervals. A 10% burn-in was discarded prior to tree summarization.

---

# **Results**

## Gene alignments and information content

The final concatenated dataset comprised six loci: three mitochondrial (12S, 16S rRNA, CYTB) and three nuclear (IRBP, vWF intron 11, vWF exon 28). Individual alignments contained:

- 12S: 397 bp, 38 unique site patterns  
- 16S: 212 bp, 44 unique site patterns  
- CYTB: 423 bp, 56 unique site patterns  
- IRBP: 604 bp, 27 unique site patterns  
- vWF exon 28: 510 bp, 26 unique site patterns  
- vWF intron 11: 567 bp, 52 unique site patterns

Mitochondrial loci were more variable, with a higher proportion of parsimony-informative sites, while the nuclear markers contributed an independent, lower-rate signal that helped stabilize deeper relationships in the species tree (Edwards, 2009).

## Overall species-tree topology and node support

The StarBEAST3 multispecies coalescent analysis recovered a fully resolved species tree for the five focal lineages: *Otolemur garnettii* (outgroup), *P. z. zanzibaricus*, *P. z. udzungwensis*, *P. rondoensis*, and the Malawi lineage (*Paragalago cf. granti/nyasae*).

All internal nodes in the MCC tree were strongly supported, with posterior probabilities ≥ 0.9998 (three nodes at 1.0 and one at 0.9998). No alternative topologies with substantial posterior weight were detected. This level of support exceeds typical thresholds for well-resolved phylogenetic relationships (Huelsenbeck & Rannala, 2004).

The inferred topology (Figure 1\) can be summarized as:

- *Otolemur garnettii* as outgroup  
- (*P. rondoensis*, (*Paragalago cf. granti/nyasae*, (*P. z. zanzibaricus*, *P. z. udzungwensis*)))

Thus, *P. rondoensis* is recovered as the earliest-diverging *Paragalago* lineage, with the Malawi lineage forming the next split, and the two *P. zanzibaricus* subspecies forming a terminal sister pair.

**Table 1\.** Summary of *Paragalago* lineages included in this study, with geographic origin, normalized relative divergence age (relative to the root \= 1.0), and posterior support from the MCC species tree.

| Lineage Name | Label Used in Tree | Geographic Region | Relative Divergence Age\* | Posterior Support |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| *P. rondoensis* | Paragalago\_rondoensis | Southern coastal forests (Rondo Plateau) | \~0.31 (deepest ingroup) | 1.00 |
| *P. cf. granti/nyasae* | Paragalago\_nyasae | Southern Malawi (Blantyre/Mulanje region) | \~0.13 | 1.00 |
| *P. z. zanzibaricus* | Paragalago\_zanzibaricus | Zanzibar Island (Unguja) | \~0.03 | 1.00 |
| *P. z. udzungwensis* | Paragalago\_zanzibaricus\_udzungwensis | Mainland Tanzania (Ruvu South, Ngumburuni) | \~0.03 | 0.99978 |
| *Otolemur garnettii* (outgroup) | Otolemur\_garnettii | East Africa (widespread; sampled Tanzania) | Root \= 1.0 | — |

**\*** Relative divergence ages expressed as:  
\[ \\text{Normalized age} \= \\frac{\\text{root height – node height}}{\\text{root height}} \]  
Thus, 0.31 ≈ oldest ingroup split; 0.03 ≈ shallowest split (Zanzibar vs. Udzungwa).

## Relative divergence depths (normalized to total tree height)

Because the analysis was not calibrated with fossil or rate priors, node ages are interpreted in **relative** rather than absolute time. Using the distance from the root to the tips as 1.0 (100% of total height), the main splits are:

- **Root (including *Otolemur*)**: normalized age \= 1.0 (by definition)  
- **MRCA of all *Paragalago* lineages** (Zanzibar, Udzungwa, Rondo, Malawi): \~0.31 of total tree height  
- **Split between *P. rondoensis* and the remaining dwarf galagos**: essentially coincident with the *Paragalago* MRCA and represents the deepest divergence within the genus  
- **MRCA of (*Paragalago cf. granti/nyasae*, *P. z. zanzibaricus*, *P. z. udzungwensis*)**: \~0.13 of total height  
- **MRCA of the two *P. zanzibaricus* subspecies (*P. z. zanzibaricus* vs. *P. z. udzungwensis*)**: \~0.03 of total height

In relative terms, this means:

- The **Rondo lineage** diverged earliest and is substantially deeper than any other split in the ingroup, consistent with its recognition as a distinct species (Honess, 1996).  
- The **Malawi lineage** diverged after *P. rondoensis* but well before the separation between Zanzibar and Udzungwa, indicating a distinct southern *Paragalago* lineage.  
- The **Zanzibar vs. Udzungwa divergence** is comparatively shallow, consistent with their current treatment as subspecies (Butynski et al., 2006).

## Placement of the Malawi lineage (*Paragalago cf. granti/nyasae*)

The two Malawi samples formed a cohesive clade with very high posterior support. This clade is sister to the Zanzibar \+ Udzungwa group rather than to *P. rondoensis*, indicating that the Malawi dwarf galagos share a more recent common ancestor with coastal and island *P. zanzibaricus* lineages than with the deeply divergent Rondo lineage.

Despite the strong genetic distinctiveness of the Malawi lineage, the lack of comparative sequences from confirmed *P. granti* populations in Mozambique and the historical synonymy of *P. nyasae* with *P. granti* (Honess, 1996\) prevent a definitive taxonomic assignment. We therefore refer to this clade as ***Paragalago cf. granti/nyasae*** and interpret it as a southern *Paragalago* lineage isolated within the Malawi miombo–montane forest system.

## Subspecies structure within the *P. zanzibaricus* complex

Within the Zanzibar complex, *P. z. zanzibaricus* (Zanzibar Island) and *P. z. udzungwensis* (mainland Tanzania) forms a terminal sister pair with maximal posterior support. The shallow relative divergence (\~3% of total tree height) indicates a more recent separation than the deeper splits involving *P. rondoensis* and the Malawi lineage. This pattern is consistent with subspecies-level differentiation driven by repeated isolation of the Zanzibar Channel during Pleistocene sea-level fluctuations (Voelker et al., 2013\) and long-term ecological stability of coastal and Eastern Arc forests (Lovett, 1993).

---

### **Figure 2\.**

**Maximum clade credibility species tree** inferred under the multispecies coalescent. Tip labels represent species or provisional lineages; node bars show 95% highest posterior density (HPD) intervals; numbers indicate posterior support values. The Malawi lineage is labelled ***Paragalago cf. granti/nyasae*** to reflect current taxonomic uncertainty.

*(Insert figure: `species_tree.svg`)*

---

# **Discussion**

Our multilocus phylogeny clarifies several outstanding questions about the evolutionary structure of *Paragalago* and provides the first molecular data for dwarf galagos from southern Malawi.

## **Phylogenetic Relationships and Taxonomic Implications**

The strongly supported topology recovered here is consistent with previous molecular phylogenies of eastern African dwarf galagos (Masters et al., 2017; Pozzi et al., 2015\) and extends geographic sampling to include the previously unsampled Malawi region. The deep divergence of *P. rondoensis* confirms its status as a distinct species (Honess, 1996; Butynski et al., 2006), and the intermediate position of the Malawi lineage suggests it may represent either *P. granti*, a distinct *P. nyasae* lineage, or a previously unrecognized taxon.

The use of the multispecies coalescent framework (StarBEAST3; Ogilvie et al., 2017\) provides a more robust estimate of species relationships than concatenated analyses by explicitly modeling gene tree–species tree discordance due to incomplete lineage sorting (Degnan & Rosenberg, 2009; Edwards, 2009). The very high posterior support (≥ 0.9998) for all nodes indicates that the recovered topology is well-supported despite the relatively modest number of loci analyzed.

## **Zanzibar and Eastern Arc Diversification**

The strong separation between *P. z. zanzibaricus* and *P. z. udzungwensis* across all loci reflects repeated isolation of the Zanzibar Channel during Pleistocene sea-level fluctuations and the long-term ecological stability of Eastern Arc refugia (Lovett, 1993; Bowie et al., 2006). The concordance between mitochondrial and nuclear markers provides robust support for recognizing these as distinct subspecies (Butynski et al., 2006).

Island–mainland divergence in East African taxa has been documented across multiple taxonomic groups, with Zanzibar populations typically showing genetic differentiation from mainland counterparts due to repeated isolation during interglacial high sea-level stands (Voelker et al., 2013). The relatively shallow divergence between *P. z. zanzibaricus* and *P. z. udzungwensis* (\~3% of total tree height) suggests recent separation, consistent with Pleistocene vicariance rather than older Miocene or Pliocene divergence events.

## **Deep Divergence of the Rondo Lineage**

*P. rondoensis* emerges as one of the most isolated dwarf galago lineages, with a divergence time substantially deeper than other ingroup splits. This pattern is consistent with the recognition of *P. rondoensis* as a distinct species based on acoustic, morphological, and ecological criteria (Honess, 1996; Butynski et al., 2006). The Rondo Plateau represents a highly fragmented coastal forest system in southern Tanzania, and the deep genetic divergence of *P. rondoensis* likely reflects long-term isolation in this region (Burgess & Clarke, 2000).

The confinement of *P. rondoensis* to heavily fragmented southern coastal forests underscores the urgent need to protect the remaining Rondo Plateau habitats. This species is currently classified as Critically Endangered due to its restricted range and ongoing habitat loss (Butynski et al., 2006).

## **Malawi Dwarf Galagos and the *granti/nyasae* Problem**

The Malawi lineage represents the first genetic data ever generated for dwarf galagos in southern Malawi. Its phylogenetic placement confirms that southern miombo systems harbour a lineage distinct from coastal and Eastern Arc forms, with a divergence depth intermediate between the deep *P. rondoensis* split and the shallow Zanzibar–Udzungwa divergence.

The historical classification of *nyasae* from Mount Mulanje (Thomas, 1908), later synonymized with *P. granti* (Honess, 1996), complicates interpretation. Our findings suggest three possibilities:

1. The Malawi lineage represents *P. granti*, and the historical *P. nyasae* holotype is conspecific with *P. granti* from Mozambique.  
2. The holotype of *nyasae* represents a local southern form distinct from *P. granti*, and the Malawi lineage sampled here corresponds to *P. nyasae*.  
3. Malawi contains a previously unrecognized population structure, with multiple lineages present across different elevational or habitat zones.

Resolving this question will require genetic sampling of confirmed *P. granti* populations from Mozambique and, ideally, ancient DNA extraction from the *P. nyasae* holotype (Thomas, 1908). Given the lack of comparative *granti* sequences and uncertainty of historical taxonomy, our conservative designation ***Paragalago cf. granti/nyasae*** is appropriate pending further sampling.

Previous nocturnal surveys in Malawi documented vocalizations consistent with *P. granti* across several sites in the region (Honess & Bearder, 1996; Courtenay & Bearder, 1989).  
However, the small sample size (n=2) and captive origin of the sequenced individuals limit our ability to assess intraspecific variation within the Malawi lineage.

## **Biogeographic Synthesis**

Our results support the view that *Paragalago* diversification has been driven by a combination of:

* **Island isolation** (Zanzibar): Repeated sea-level fluctuations during Pleistocene glacial cycles isolated Zanzibar from the mainland, promoting genetic divergence (Voelker et al., 2013).  
* **Montane refugia** (Eastern Arc): Long-term stability of Eastern Arc forests provided refugia during Pleistocene aridification, maintaining distinct mainland populations (Lovett, 1993; Bowie et al., 2006).  
* **Coastal forest fragmentation** (Rondo Plateau): Fragmentation of coastal forests in southern Tanzania isolated *P. rondoensis* populations, leading to deep genetic divergence (Burgess & Clarke, 2000).  
* **Southern miombo woodland persistence** (Malawi): Persistence of miombo–forest mosaics in southern Malawi maintained a distinct southern lineage, potentially representing *P. granti* or *P. nyasae* (White, 1983).

These geographically isolated ecosystems have produced genetically distinct lineages that together represent a key component of East Africa's nocturnal primate diversity (Bearder et al., 2003; Butynski et al., 2006).

The biogeographic patterns recovered here are consistent with broader patterns of diversification in East African forest-dependent taxa, where Pleistocene climatic oscillations drove repeated cycles of forest expansion and contraction, promoting allopatric divergence in isolated refugia (Lovett, 1993; Bowie et al., 2006). The Eastern Arc Mountains and coastal forests of Tanzania have been identified as important centers of endemism and refugia for forest-dependent species (Burgess & Clarke, 2000), and our results confirm that dwarf galagos exhibit similar biogeographic structuring.

## **Conservation Implications**

The phylogenetic and biogeographic patterns documented here have important conservation implications. Each of the four lineages identified (*P. rondoensis*, *P. cf. granti/nyasae*, *P. z. zanzibaricus*, *P. z. udzungwensis*) represents a distinct evolutionary unit that should be considered separately in conservation planning (Moritz, 1994).

*P. rondoensis* is currently classified as Critically Endangered due to its restricted range and ongoing habitat loss in the Rondo Plateau region (Butynski et al., 2006). Our results confirm the deep genetic distinctiveness of this lineage, reinforcing the urgency of protecting remaining Rondo coastal forest fragments.

The Malawi lineage, while not yet formally assessed, likely faces similar threats from habitat loss and fragmentation in southern Malawi. Miombo woodlands and associated forest patches in this region are under increasing pressure from agricultural expansion and charcoal production (White, 1983). Further surveys are needed to assess the distribution, population size, and conservation status of this lineage.

The Zanzibar and mainland Tanzania lineages, while less threatened than *P. rondoensis*, also face ongoing habitat loss from agricultural expansion and urbanization (Butynski et al., 2006). Conservation of coastal and Eastern Arc forest fragments is essential for maintaining the evolutionary diversity of *Paragalago*.

## **Limitations and Future Directions**

Several limitations of this study should be acknowledged. First, the sample size for the Malawi lineage is small (n=2), and both individuals were obtained from captive sources. While field surveys suggest that these individuals are representative of a broader regional population (Honess & Bearder, 1996), additional sampling from wild populations across southern Malawi is needed to assess intraspecific variation and confirm the geographic extent of this lineage.

Second, the lack of comparative sequences from confirmed *P. granti* populations in Mozambique prevents definitive taxonomic assignment of the Malawi lineage. Future studies should prioritize sampling from the type locality of *P. granti* in Mozambique (Thomas & Wroughton, 1907\) to resolve the relationship between Malawi and Mozambique populations.

Third, the phylogenetic analysis presented here is based on six loci (three mitochondrial, three nuclear), which is modest by current phylogenomic standards. While the multispecies coalescent framework provides robust estimates of species relationships even with limited loci (Edwards, 2009), future studies employing genome-wide SNP data or whole-genome sequencing would provide higher resolution and allow investigation of potential introgression or hybridization among lineages.

Fourth, the analysis was not calibrated with fossil or biogeographic constraints, limiting our ability to estimate absolute divergence times. While relative divergence depths provide useful insights into the temporal sequence of diversification events, future studies incorporating fossil calibrations or molecular clock constraints would allow more precise dating of divergence events and comparison with paleoclimatic data.

Finally, ancient DNA extraction from the *P. nyasae* holotype (Thomas, 1908\) would provide direct evidence for the taxonomic identity of the Malawi lineage. While ancient DNA extraction from museum specimens is challenging, recent advances in ancient DNA methodology make this approach increasingly feasible (Raxworthy & Smith, 2021).

---

# **Acknowledgements**

This research was made possible through the collaboration, support, and guidance of many individuals and institutions.

We thank the Tanzania Wildlife Research Institute (TAWIRI), the Forestry and Beekeeping Division, and the Tanzania Commission for Science and Technology (COSTECH) for research permissions and field support. The Tanzania Forest Conservation Group (TFCG) provided essential logistical assistance and local expertise that enabled successful work in coastal and Eastern Arc forests.

For Malawi fieldwork and contextual knowledge, we express deep gratitude to Tiwonge Mzumara, the staff of Likhubula House, Françoise Dowsett-Lemaire, Ken Rice (with Zit and Loopy), Graham Wallace, Julian Bayliss, John Wondale, and the staff of the Tea Research Foundation. Their contributions enriched the study substantially.

We thank the Natural History Museum, London, for access to historical records of the *P. nyasae* holotype.

Finally, we acknowledge Simon K. Bearder for his expertise in acoustic identification, taxonomy, and nocturnal survey methodology. His participation was critical for interpreting both Tanzanian and Malawian dwarf galago diversity.

---

# **Funding Statement**

Fieldwork and laboratory components of this project were supported by the following grants:

* **Primate Conservation Inc. (PCI)**: "Molecular Phylogeny of East African Dwarf Galagos" — USD 3,600  
* **National Geographic Committee for Research and Exploration**: "Molecular Phylogeny of East African Dwarf Galagos" — USD 19,525  
* **Conservation International — Primate Action Fund**: "A Survey of Nocturnal Primates in Malawi" — USD 1,700

Additional institutional support was provided by Oxford Brookes University and collaborating conservation organizations in Tanzania and Malawi.

---

# **Ethics Statement**

All research procedures were conducted in accordance with ethical guidelines for primate research. Tissue samples from Tanzania were collected under permits issued by the Tanzania Wildlife Research Institute (TAWIRI) and the Tanzania Commission for Science and Technology (COSTECH). Malawi samples were obtained from captive individuals kept as pets, with permission from local authorities. No animals were harmed during sample collection, and all procedures followed non-invasive protocols for tissue sampling from small primates (Bearder et al., 2003).

---

# **Data Availability Statement**

DNA sequence alignments and phylogenetic tree files are available from the corresponding author upon reasonable request. Sequences will be deposited in GenBank upon acceptance of the manuscript.

---

# **MCMC Convergence and Posterior Diagnostics**

All StarBEAST3 runs were examined in **Tracer 1.7** (Rambaut et al., 2018). Effective sample sizes (ESS) exceeded 200 for all key parameters, including tree likelihood, substitution rates, clock rates, and population size estimates. Posterior traces showed stable mixing with no evidence of non-stationarity.

The species tree posterior distribution was summarised using **TreeAnnotator**, selecting the maximum clade credibility (MCC) tree, including mean node heights, and applying a 10% burn-in. Node bars represent 95% highest posterior density intervals.

---

# **Supplementary Material**

* Aligned FASTA files  
* BEAST XML configuration file  
* MCC tree (species\_tree.svg)  
* Tracer log summaries  
* Field survey notes (Malawi 2009\)

---

# **Funding**

This research was funded by the National Geographic Society Committee for Research and Exploration (Grant No. 8925-11, Principal Investigator: Simon K. Bearder).

We thank the Malawi High Commission for research authorization (reference LC/2/2/24). We thank the Wildlife Division of Tanzania for issuing CITES export permits (permit 24905). We thank local communities and field assistants in Tanzania and Malawi for their support during fieldwork.

### **Table S1. Sampling localities included in this study**

| Lineage | Locality | Country | Latitude | Longitude | Notes |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| *Paragalago zanzibaricus zanzibaricus* | Kiwengwa Forest Reserve | Tanzania | **6.0056°S** | **39.3770°E** | Zanzibar Island population |
| *P. z. udzungwensis* | Ruvu South Forest Reserve | Tanzania | **6.9669°S** | **38.8628°E** | Coastal forest reserve |
| *P. z. udzungwensis* | Ngumburuni Forest Reserve | Tanzania | **7.8872°S** | **39.0489°E** | Southern coastal forest reserve |
| *Paragalago cf. granti/nyasae* | Blantyre region | Malawi | **16.0526°S** | **35.3713°E** | Source of captive individuals |
| *Paragalago rondoensis* | Rondo Plateau | Tanzania | **10.0810°S** | **39.2750°E** | Reference locality |
